task: I’m trying to turn one brand concept into a few different social posts without making each version feel like a full redesign. What’s the simplest way to set that up so I can move fast and keep the visuals consistent?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — the simplest way is to build **one master system** and then swap only a few controlled elements.

## Set it up like this
### 1. Lock the core
Keep these the same across every post:
- layout structure
- type hierarchy
- color palette
- logo placement
- image treatment
- tone of voice

### 2. Create swap zones
Only vary:
- headline
- supporting line
- hero image or crop
- CTA
- one accent element if needed

### 3. Use one base template
Make one clean master layout, then duplicate it for each post so you’re not redesigning from scratch.

### 4. Limit the variation
Each version should feel like a **different angle**, not a different brand.

## Practical rule
If changing something makes the post feel like a new identity instead of a new message, it’s too much.

## Fastest workflow
- build master concept
- define 3–5 content variations
- swap only copy and crop
- keep the system intact
- export in a batch

## What keeps it consistent
- same grid
- same margins
- same type scale
- same image style
- same spacing logic

If you want, I can turn this into a **simple social post system with a master layout and 3 variation types**.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, direction’s right, but it’s still a little too generic.

What I need is the actual system breakdown — like:
- what the master template is
- which 2–4 things are allowed to change
- how many post variants you’d realistically spin from one concept
- and what stays locked so it doesn’t drift

Basically, give me a clean template logic I can hand to a designer or build myself without it turning into a whole new art direction every time.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — the cleanest way is to treat it like a **modular system**, not a set of separate posts.

## Master template
Build **one base layout** that never changes:
- grid
- margins
- type scale
- logo placement
- color system
- image treatment
- overall pacing

That’s the shell.

## What can change
Limit it to **2–4 variables**:
1. **Headline**
2. **Supporting line**
3. **Hero image / crop**
4. **CTA or label**

If you need a fifth variable, it should be something small like an accent color or icon — not a structural change.

## What stays locked
Keep these fixed across all versions:
- layout structure
- hierarchy
- font pairing
- spacing logic
- brand colors
- logo position
- visual tone
- treatment of photography or texture

If any of those change, the system starts to feel like a new concept.

## How many variants to make
Usually **3 to 5** is enough from one concept:
- **1 core version**
- **2–3 message variations**
- **1 lighter or more direct version**

That gives you range without multiplying work.

## Simple rule
If a change forces you to rethink the layout, it’s not a variant — it’s a new design.

## Best workflow
1. Lock the master template
2. Define the allowed swap points
3. Build the first version
4. Duplicate only within the system
5. Review for drift before export

## Hand-off version
You can tell a designer:

**Keep the structure fixed. Only swap headline, subline, crop, and CTA. Anything else stays locked.**

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page template rule set** you could paste directly into a brief.

user_simulator.text:
Closer. I’d want the actual framework tightened into something I can hand off without people freelancing the art direction.

Like:

**Master template =** one locked composition with a fixed grid, fixed type scale, fixed logo zone, fixed color family, fixed image treatment. Same bones every time.

**Allowed to change =**
- headline
- subhead / support line
- hero image crop or swap
- CTA / label

That’s it. Maybe one tiny accent move if the campaign needs it, but not a redesign lever.

**Variant count =** 3–5 max from one concept.
- 1 hero / flagship post
- 2–3 message-led variations
- 1 punchier or more utility version

Anything beyond that and it starts feeling like you’re building a new campaign, not a system.

**Locked so it doesn’t drift =**
- layout structure
- margins / spacing logic
- font pairing
- hierarchy
- logo placement
- brand colors
- image style / texture treatment
- tone of motion, if there’s animation

**Rule of thumb:** if a change makes the designer reconsider composition, it’s not a variable. It’s outside the system.

Still a little generic though — I’d want you to go one step further and turn it into a clean production rule set, like: what’s fixed at the brand level, what’s fixed at the campaign level, and what’s allowed to flex per post.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s the cleaner production rule set.

## 1) Brand level: never move
These are fixed across everything:
- logo usage
- brand colors
- type family
- type scale
- image treatment
- spacing system
- overall tone

If these change, it’s not a variant anymore.

## 2) Campaign level: locked for the set
These stay consistent across the whole post series:
- composition template
- grid
- hierarchy
- motion style
- CTA style
- framing logic
- visual mood

This is what makes the posts feel like one campaign, not separate ideas.

## 3) Post level: allowed to flex
Only these should change per post:
- headline
- subhead
- hero image or crop
- label / CTA
- one small accent, if needed

That’s the full flex zone.

## Practical rule
If a change affects:
- layout
- hierarchy
- spacing
- typography decisions
- or the visual system itself

…it’s not a post variation. It’s a new direction.

## Production rule
**Brand stays fixed. Campaign stays consistent. Post content flexes.**

## Good handoff line
**Keep the brand system locked. Keep the campaign template locked. Only flex the message and the image.**

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page studio rule sheet** with headings you could paste straight into a deck or brief.

user_simulator.text:
Closer, but still a little too high-level. I need the rule sheet to read like an actual production doc, not just a principle.

Give it to me in a tighter structure like:

**BRAND LOCKED**
- logo rules
- type family / scale
- color system
- image treatment
- spacing system
- tone

**CAMPAIGN LOCKED**
- composition template
- grid
- hierarchy
- motion style
- CTA style
- framing logic
- visual mood

**POST FLEX**
- headline
- subhead
- hero crop / image swap
- label / CTA
- optional tiny accent

And I’d want one more line under each section saying what absolutely cannot move. Like, not just “locked,” but what counts as drift.

Also, call out the max variant count in the doc itself — 3 to 5 per concept, no more — so nobody starts stretching it into a whole content farm.

If you want, just write it as a clean handoff sheet I can paste straight into a brief.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a cleaner handoff sheet you can paste straight into a brief.

---

# SOCIAL VARIANT SYSTEM

## BRAND LOCKED
These do not change across the set:
- logo rules
- type family / scale
- color system
- image treatment
- spacing system
- tone

**Drift =** any change that makes the post feel like a different brand, not a different message.

---

## CAMPAIGN LOCKED
These stay fixed across the full series:
- composition template
- grid
- hierarchy
- motion style
- CTA style
- framing logic
- visual mood

**Drift =** any change that forces a new layout, new pacing, or a new campaign look.

---

## POST FLEX
These are the only elements allowed to vary per post:
- headline
- subhead
- hero crop / image swap
- label / CTA
- optional tiny accent

**Drift =** anything that changes the structure, hierarchy, or visual system.

---

## VARIANT COUNT
**3 to 5 posts max per concept.**  
Anything beyond that starts becoming a new campaign, not a controlled variation set.

---

## RULE OF THUMB
If a change makes the designer reconsider composition, it is not a flex item.  
It is outside the system.

---

If you want, I can also turn this into a **more polished studio version** or a **more blunt internal production version**.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah — that’s basically the shape I wanted. Clean enough to hand off, and the drift language is useful.

If anything, I’d probably want one more pass later with a slightly more production-looking version — like less “brand/campaign/post” and more actual studio shorthand — but this is solid. Works.
